coredata.dc:title: Colorimetric detection of 1,5-anhydroglucitol based on graphene quantum dots and enzyme-catalyzed reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhide
Zhao, Le
Wang, Zhihong
Xue, Wen
Wang, Yunxiao
Huang, Yong
Liang, Jintao
Chen, Jiejing
Li, Guiyin
coredata.dc:description: Early diagnosis of diabetes yields significant clinical benefits. The serum level of 1,5‑anhydroglucitol (1,5‑AG) has been a new biochemical marker for postprandial hyperglycemia. In this study, a simple colorimetric method for 1,5‑AG detection has been designed based on highly efficient peroxidase mimetic activity of GQDs and enzyme-catalyzed reaction. By the catalytic action of pyranose oxidase (PROD), the 1,5‑AG was oxidized to 1,5‑anhydrofuctose and H2O2. The GQDs in the presence of H2O2 exhibited highly efficient catalytic activity toward the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′‑tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue colored product. The influence of relevant experimental variables was optimized. A linear relationship of optical signal with the concentration of 1,5‑AG in the range of 20.0–100.0μg/mL with the regression correlation coefficient of 0.9985 was obtained which could be monitored by colorimetry detection. The limit of detection (LOD) for 1,5‑AG detection was approximately 0.144μg/mL. All in all, the proposed 1,5‑AG detection system based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction showed better performances with simple operation, low-cost, higher selectivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 1,5‑anhydroglucitol
Graphene quantum dots
Colorimetric detection
Pyranose oxidase
Enzyme-catalyzed reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design principle of colorimetric detection of 1,5‑AG
Peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of GQDs for reduction of H2O2
Optimization of experimental conditions for 1,5‑AG detection
Analytical performance for the detection of 1,5‑AG

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81760534 and 81460451 ), the National Science Foundation of Guangxi province of China (Nos. 2015GXNSFDA139025 , 2016GXNSFAA380011 and 2016GXNSFAA380080 ), the Appropriate Health Technology Development Project of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (No. 201422-03 ) and the Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research (Nos. 2015-181 h-01 and 2015-181h-02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–visible spectra of (a) GQDs solution and (b) GO solution. (B) Photoluminescence spectrum of GQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 280nm to 330nm. (C) TEM images of the synthesized GQDs (20000X). (D) TEM images of the synthesized GQDs (50000X). (E) Raman spectra of (a) GO and (b) GQDs. (F) Zeta potential distribution profile of the synthesized GQDs.

(A) UV–visible spectra of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs+TMB+PROD, (c) GQDs+TMB+PROD+1,5‑AG (100.0μg/mL) and (d) GQDs+TMB+H2O2 (25.0μmol/L). (B) The calibration curves of H2O2. Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements.

(A) The effect of the ratios of GQDs to 1,5‑AG on the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction. (B) The effect of reaction temperature of GQDs+TMB+1.5‑AG+PROD on the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction. (C) The effect of pH on the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction. (D) The effect of ion strength of the buffer solution on the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction. Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements.

(A) Optical picture of GQDs+TMB+PROD and interaction with different concentration of 1,5‑AG (a) 20.0μg/mL, (b) 40.0μg/mL, (c) 60.0μg/mL, (d) 80.0μg/mL, (e) 100.0μg/mL. (B) UV–vis spectra of GQDs+TMB+PROD and interaction with different concentration of 1,5‑AG. (C) The calibration curves of 1,5‑AG. (D) Selectivity of 1,5‑AG detection. All measurements were performed in 200μL testing system (pH8.6, 0.1mol/L citric acid and 0.2mol/L NaH2PO4) mixing with GQDs, PROD (1.0mg/mL) and TMB (1.0mg/mL). Error bars represent the standard deviation for three measurements.
Principle of colorimetric detection of 1,5‑AG based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction.
Sensing characteristics of the proposed methods along with those reported in literature.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of 1,5‑AG in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Colorimetric detection of 1,5-anhydroglucitol based on graphene quantum dots and enzyme-catalyzed reaction

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: G. Li, School of Life and Environmental Sciences, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, Guangxi 541004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life and Environmental Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Early diagnosis of diabetes yields significant clinical benefits. The serum level of 1,5‑anhydroglucitol (1,5‑AG) has been a new biochemical marker for postprandial hyperglycemia. In this study, a simple colorimetric method for 1,5‑AG detection has been designed based on highly efficient peroxidase mimetic activity of GQDs and enzyme-catalyzed reaction. By the catalytic action of pyranose oxidase (PROD), the 1,5‑AG was oxidized to 1,5‑anhydrofuctose and H2O2. The GQDs in the presence of H2O2 exhibited highly efficient catalytic activity toward the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′‑tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to a blue colored product. The influence of relevant experimental variables was optimized. A linear relationship of optical signal with the concentration of 1,5‑AG in the range of 20.0–100.0μg/mL with the regression correlation coefficient of 0.9985 was obtained which could be monitored by colorimetry detection. The limit of detection (LOD) for 1,5‑AG detection was approximately 0.144μg/mL. All in all, the proposed 1,5‑AG detection system based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction showed better performances with simple operation, low-cost, higher selectivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a common chronic disease with high blood sugar levels over a prolonged period, and may cause long-term damage or failure of various organs such as heart, kidney, eyes, and blood vessels [1,2]. The glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level is the gold standard of glycemic control in clinic, however, HbA1c only reflects the time-averaged glucose levels in the past 2–3months and cannot response the changes of glycemia in a dynamic manner [3,4]. Recently, 1,5‑Anhydroglucitol (1,5‑AG), 1-deoxy form of glucose, has been an important biomarker for postprandial hyperglycemia and short-term glucose control over the prior 24h–2weeks [5,6]. 1,5‑AG is a six‑carbon chain monosaccharide in human serum. The half-life of 1,5‑AG is 1–2weeks and the major excretion route is renal [7,8]. Serum 1,5‑AG levels can not only reflect the blood glucose of diabetic patients over the previous 1–2weeks, but also offer the advantage of representing postprandial glucose in the therapy effect of antidiabetic medication in a dynamic manner [9,10]. To date, 1,5‑AG was measured using enzymatic method, chromatography or mass spectrometry techniques [9,11–14]. Serum 1,5‑AG level determination was commercially approved by the United States Federal Drug Administration in 2003, marketed as Glycomark™ assay [13,15]. These methods are sensitive and precise, but there are a few disadvantages for its expensive equipment, complex sample pretreatment, complicated operation and time consuming. Therefore, the fast, simple and accurate method to detect 1,5‑AG has profound clinical applications.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a newly zero-dimensional material of sp2-hybridized carbon derived from graphene, is constituted of one or few-layered graphene nanosheets with lateral diameters less than 100nm [16,17]. GQDs possess huge number of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, large surface-to-volume ratio, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility and good electrical conductivity [18–20]. Hence, GQDs have a variety of applications in biological imaging, optical sensor, light-emitting diode, optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, and biosensing [21–24]. Similar to the carbon nanomaterials like single walled carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, carbon dots and graphene dots [25–27], GQDs possess a higher intrinsic peroxidase-like activity to reduce hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) due to the intact aromatic structure and rich periphery carboxylic groups. Consequently, GQDs are probable to be used for H2O2 detection [19,28–30]. Ye Zhu et al. [28] have fabricated non-enzymatic H2O2 biosensor based on polydopamine interconnected graphene quantum dots and gold nanoparticles (Au-PDA-GQD). The synergistic effect of Au-PDA-GQD nanocomposite presented an excellent peroxidase-like activity. The H2O2 biosensor possessed better sensing performances with wide linear range (0.1–40μM and 40–20,000μM) and a low detection limit (5.8nM). Particularly, the peroxidase mimetic activity of GQDs can promote the effective oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′‑tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in presence of H2O2 and generate colored oxidized form. The colorimetric signal of the catalytic reaction can be monitored spectrophotrometrically or visually [31,32].

In the present study, GQDs, an enzyme mimetic of peroxidase, have been used for detection of 1,5‑AG. In this assay, a H2O2 by-product was detected colorimetrically after the oxidation of 1,5‑AG to 1,5‑anhydrofuctose by pyranose oxidase (PROD). The GQDs in the presence of H2O2 exhibited highly efficient catalytic activity toward the oxidation of peroxidase substrate, TMB, to produce a colored product. Monitoring the maximum absorbance change of GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction system allows us to quantify 1,5‑AG in human serum. The effects of the ratios of GQDs to1,5‑AG, pH, temperature and ion strength were assessed, as well as the specificity and reproducibility were discussed.

Graphene oxide (GO) were purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). 1,5‑AG and PROD were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA), N‑(3‑dimethylaminopropyl)‑N′‑ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 30% H2O2 and 3,3′,5,5′‑tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. (Shanghai, China). Galactose (Gal), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) and dopamine hydrochloride (Dop) were supplied by Xilong Chemical Co. (Shanghai, China). All other reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water of 18MΩ·cm purified from Milli-Q purification system (Milli-Pore, Bedford, MA, USA). The human blood serum was acquired from 181st Hospital of Chinese People's Liberation Army (Guilin, China).

PROD solution (1.0mg/mL) was prepared with glycine buffer solution (0.05mol/L, pH8.6). TMB solution (1.0mg/mL, dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide) was used to prepare the peroxidase substrate solution. The supporting buffer was composed of 0.1mol/L citric acid and 0.2mol/L hydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), and the pH was adjusted to 8.6.

The GQDs was synthesized by the hydrothermal method according to the previously reported procedure with minor modification [33]. Briefly, 0.05g GO was treated in the mixture of 10mL H2SO4 and 30mL HNO3 for 10h under mild ultrasonication (500W, 40Hz). The mixture was diluted with 100mL ultrapure water under ice-water bath, and then the solid product was collected by centrifugation for 10min with 6000rpm. The sediment was redispersed in 40mL ultrapure water and adjusted pH to 8–9 with ammonia solution. Following that, the suspension was heated at 200°C for 12h in a poly-(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtrated with 0.22μm microporous membrane and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (cutoff molecular weight 3500Da) for 48h. Finally, the obtained GQDs were stored at 4°C.

UV–visible spectroscopy of GQDs solution was performed with UH5300 UV–Visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies Corporation, Japan). The morphologies were observed with JEOL transmission electron microscopy (JEM-2100F, Japan). Raman spectra were measured on a DXR Raman microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments Ltd., United Kingdom). Fluorescence spectra were measured with a F-4600 fluorescent spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies Corporation, Japan).

For H2O2 detection, the following was added sequentially to a 1.0mL Falcon conical centrifuge tubes: 10μL of the as-prepared GQDs solution were added to 150μL of buffer solution (pH8.6, 0.1mol/L citric acid and 0.2mol/L NaH2PO4), in which contained 20μL of different concentrations of H2O2. Then, 20μL of TMB (1.0mg/mL) was added to the above reaction mixture and followed 1h incubation at 30°C. The maximum absorbance of the oxidized TMB was recorded at 652nm. The final concentrations of H2O2 in the system varied from 10.0 to 30.0μmol/L.

1,5‑AG detection based on GQDs and enzyme-catalyzed reaction was done as follows: Firstly, 10μL PROD solution (1.0mg/mL) and 10μL different concentrations of 1,5‑AG in glycine buffer solution (0.05mol/L, pH8.6) were incubated in a 1.0mL Falcon conical centrifuge tube at 37°C for 30min [34]. Then, 10μL GQDs solution and 150μL of supporting buffer solution (pH8.6, 0.1mol/L citric acid and 0.2mol/L NaH2PO4) were added into the above solution. Finally, 20μL TMB (1.0mg/mL) was added to the above reaction mixture and followed 1h incubation at 30°C. The product color of the oxidized TMB was monitored at 652nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principle of colorimetric detection of 1,5‑AG
ce:section.ce:para:
In recent years, colorimetric strategy has drawn much attention owing to its convenience in simple and rapid visual detection without the aid of any other advanced instruments [31,35]. The analytical principle for colorimetric detection of 1,5‑AG based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction was depicted in Scheme 1 . Firstly, the PROD catalyzed the oxidation of 1,5‑AG to produce 1,5‑anhydrofuctose and H2O2. Then, GQDs catalyzed the H2O2-mediated oxidation peroxidase substrate TMB, resulting a color change. The colorless TMB was oxidized into blue oxidized TMB in the presence of H2O2 by the assistance of GQDs. The reactions can be explained by the following equations:
1 , 5 ‐ AG + 2
H 2
O +
O 2
→ PROD
1 , 5 ‐ anhydrofuctose + 2
H 2
O 2
2
H 2
O 2
+ TMB
→
GQD S
2
H 2
O +
O 2
+
TMB OX

The catalytic mechanism probably originates from the following aspects. On the one hand, a higher peroxidase-like activity of GQDs plays a key role in the reduction of H2O2 due to the small size of GQDs, which have a higher density of functional groups and unpaired electrons on its edge. On the other hand, an acceleration of electron transfer occurred from top of the valence band of GQDs into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of H2O2 because of GQDs with n-type incorporation of charge transfer process. In addition, an acceleration of electron transfer occurred from the GQDs to H2O2 due to the electron transfer from lone-pair electrons in TMB amino groups to the GQDs because of an increase in the electron density and mobility in the GQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs in water was shown in Fig. 1A. A broad absorption profile is observed with the presence of a peak at around 275nm which can be assigned to the typical absorption of energy in the electronic π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains [27]. The photographs of the as-prepared GQDs taken under visible light and UV light can also be observed in Inset of Fig. 1A. The synthesized GQDs solution was a pale yellow product under the normal daylight conditions. While a bright blue fluorescence was observed when the GQDs were irradiated with a UV light, which can be attributed to the presence of abundant sp2 domains and CO functional groups in the GQDs [36].

The GQDs excitation-dependent luminescent behavior was demonstrated by registering the emission peaks shifts as a result of the excitation wavelength applied from 280 to 330nm (Fig. 1B). The photoluminescence of GQDs displayed excitation dependent behavior and a strong emission peak was observed at around 420nm. The excitation wavelength independent emission behavior can be explained by the uneven size distribution of the particles or the different emissive states from the functional groups. The defects trapped onto the surface along with the intrinsic quantum sized zigzag sites having triplet carbine ground state can be highlighted as prominent factors responsible for luminescence in GQDs [24,37]. Fig. 1C and Fig. 1D showed the TEM images of the synthesized GQDs. Near-spherical morphology was evident, and the GQDs were well dispersed with the uniform diameter in the range of 10–15nm in size.

Raman spectroscopy is a fast, nondestructive, and higher solution tool for the characterization of the graphene-based materials [38–40]. Raman spectrum of the synthesized GQDs was collected by exciting the samples with a 514nm laser (Fig. 1E). Seen from the Fig.1E, Two main characteristic Raman bands can be seen obviously at around 1350cm−1 (D band) and 1590cm−1 (G band). D band was associated with the disorder induced scattering, which resulted from the defects produced in sp2 hybridized termination plane of disordered carbonaceous material. G band was attributed to the E2g mode of graphite and corresponded to vibrations of sp2 rings in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice of GQDs [18,36]. The intensity ratio of D band to G band (ID/IG) is a useful value to estimate the amount of defect and disorder. The GQDs presented the typical Raman signals with the relative ID/IG ratio of 0.92, lower than that of GO (1.23), which verified a crystalline nature and the presence of lower disorders and structural defects in the structure of the GQDs.

The zeta potential of the GQDs was measured for the analysis of particle surface charges (Fig. 1F). The dominant peak at −70.2mV indicated the hydrophilic water-stable GQDs with negative surface due to the presence of –COOH functional groups at the surface of GQDs [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity of GQDs for reduction of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of colorimetric 1,5‑AG detection based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction was evaluated at different system with citric acid-phosphate buffer solutions and TMB (1.0mg/mL). Fig. 2A showed the UV–Visible spectra of (a) GQDs+TMB, (b) GQDs+TMB+PROD, (c) GQDs+TMB+PROD+1.5‑AG and (d) GQDs+TMB+H2O2. Seen from Fig. 2A, the typical absorbance peak of oxidation in different system was the same at 652nm. The absorbance intensity at 652nm of GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system (curve d) was much higher than that of the GQDs-TMB system (curve a). This may be the reason that GQDs had a higher peroxidase-like activity to decompose H2O2 molecules, and produced highly reactive hydroxyl radicals that oxidized TMB to TMBox (the oxidation of TMB), which was reflected on the color change of the reaction mixture from nearly colorless to bright blue. In addition, absorbance intensity at 652nm of the GQDs-TMB-PROD-1,5‑AG system (curve c) was much higher than that of the GQDs-TMB (curve a) and GQDs-TMB-PROD (curve b) systems, while lower than that of the GQDs-TMB-H2O2 system (curve d). This is due to the PROD can catalyze the oxidation of 1,5‑AG, producing H2O2, which can oxidize TMB to TMBox, resulting the higher absorbance intensity at 652nm. Such a color change made the colorimetric assay for 1,5‑AG detection to become possible.

The peroxidase-like activity of the GQDs was evaluated by catalytic oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 2B, the absorbance intensity at 652nm increased dramatically as the concentration of H2O2 increased from 10.0 to 30.0μmol/L. The regression equation was Y=0.00429×-0.02032 (Y was the absorbance and X indicated the concentration of H2O2) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9981. This GQDs based method can detect H2O2 at concentration as low as 0.071μmol/L. GQDs have intact aromatic structure and rich periphery carboxylic groups which might be ascribed to its peroxidase-like activity [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions for 1,5‑AG detection
ce:section.ce:para: H2O2 is the main product after the oxidation of 1,5‑AG to 1,5‑anhydrofuctose by PROD in the presence of oxygen. Therefore, 1,5‑AG detection could be realized by coupling GQDs peroxidase catalytic methods with PROD catalytic 1,5‑AG oxidation. To make the maximum response capacity of colorimetric detection of 1,5‑AG, the experimental conditions, such as the ratios of GQDs to1,5‑AG, pH, temperature, ion strength and so on, were optimized.

The effect of the ratios of GQDs to 1,5‑AG on the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction was appraised and the results were shown in Fig. 3A. The ratios of GQDs to 1,5‑AG were optimized by fixing the volume of 1,5‑AG (100μg/mL) at 10μL and then changing the volume of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the absorbance of GQDs-based catalytic reaction increased with increasing the volume ratio of GQDs to 1,5‑AG. The slope of this plot tended to be decreased at the volume ratio of 1.0 because of the relative poor catalytic efficiency. Therefore, we chose 1.0 as the optimized the volume ratio of GQDs to 1,5‑AG.

Secondly, the influence of reaction temperature of GQDs+TMB+1.5‑AG+PROD was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 3B. As shown in Fig. 3B, the catalytic activity of GQDs was significantly dependent on the reaction temperature. The absorbance increased rapidly with temperature increase from 25 to 30°C and then reached its highest value at 30°C. However, an obvious decline in absorbance was observed when temperature increased from 30 to 45°C. Thus, 30°C was chosen as the optimum temperature for the GQDs-based catalytic reaction.

To investigate the effect of pH on the GQDs-based catalytic reaction, the absorbance intensities of the system with 1,5‑AG, GQDs and TMB in the supporting buffer solution (0.1mol/L citric acid and 0.2mol/L NaH2PO4) with different pH (from 7.0–10.0) were detected. As shown in Fig. 3C, the catalytic activity of GQDs was affected by pH values. The catalytic oxidation of TMB with H2O2 using GQDs was much faster in alkaline solutions, and the optimum pH was 8.6.

The ion strength of the buffer is a key factor for the sensing system, since the ion strength may affect the catalytic activity of GQDs. Fig. 3D showed the effect of ion strength of the buffer (from 0.05mol/L to 0.4mol/L of NaH2PO4) on the absorbance intensity. Seen from Fig. 3D, the absorbance increased with the increasing of ion strength from 0.05 to 0.2mol/L, and then, the absorbance decreased with further increasing ion strength, implying that the ion strength is a compromise condition for the GQDs-based catalytic reaction. Therefore, 0.2mol/L of NaH2PO4 (mixing with 0.1mol/L citric acid, pH8.6) was chosen as the optimum supporting buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for the detection of 1,5‑AG
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the 1,5‑AG detection was performed by coupling GQDs with PROD enzyme catalytic 1,5‑AG oxidation. The absorbance intensity with the addition of 1,5‑AG into the system for different concentrations was recorded in Fig. 4A and Fig. 4B. The absorbance graph clearly showed that the absorbance increased the increasing concentrations of 1,5‑AG, indicating the increased rate of interaction between the reactants. Fig. 4C showed the absorbance peak signal with the change of 1,5‑AG concentrations. A linear curve was obtained between relative absorbance signal and concentrations in the range of 20.0–100.0μg/mL. The regression equation was Y=0.000547×+0.01809 (Y was the absorbance and X indicated the concentration of 1,5‑AG) with a correlation coefficient of 0.99854. Lower detection limits (LOD) can be calculated based on the standard deviation of the response (SD) and the slope (B) of the calibration curve (Fig. 4C) according to the formula: LOD=3(SD/B). SD can be estimated by the standard deviation of Y-intercepts of regression lines [32]. The LOD for 1,5‑AG detection is calculated as 0.144μg/mL. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for six replicate measurements of 100.0μg/mL1,5‑AG was 4.23%, which proved the reproducibility of the method.

Besides, the linear range and detection limit of some other methods reported earlier were summarized in Table 1 . It can be seen that the proposed colorimetric method for 1,5‑AG detection based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction offered an appropriate linear range, a low LOD and simple procedure.

To evaluate the selectivity of the present method, selective detection of 1,5‑AG relative to several foreign species such as galactose (Gal), urea acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA) and dopamine hydrochloride (Dop) with the same concentration of 100μg/mL was evaluated. The colorimetric responses of the solutions containing 1,5‑AG or foreign substance or the mixture of 1,5‑AG and foreign substance were examined under the same condition. As shown in Fig. 4D, the colorimetric absorbance intensity with 1,5‑AG, Gal, UA, AA and Dop were 0.062, 0.024, 0.022, 0.025 and 0.024, respectively. The colorimetric absorbance intensity remained nearly unchanged when the foreign substances were added solely or totally, which implied that these compounds produced little interference when the distractors were used to replace 1,5‑AG. Thus the proposed system could achieve 1,5‑AG specific determination against the potentially co-existing substance mentioned above, which proved its good selectivity.

In order to confirm the application feasibility of the proposed platform to detect 1,5‑AG in biological environment, standard addition method was utilized to determine 1,5‑AG in human serum samples. For this purpose, 0.5mL of human whole blood were taken and added into the supporting buffer solution (0.2mol/L of NaH2PO4, mixing with 0.1mol/L citric acid, pH8.6) to reach a total volume of 5mL. Then 5μL of them was added in the 200μL testing system mixing with GQDs, PROD and TMB. After that, 5μL of different concentration of standard 1,5‑AG (40.0, 50.0, 60.0μg/mL) were added into the mixture to prepare a variety of spiked samples. The corresponding results obtained by colorimetric absorbance measurements were listed in Table 2 . The average recoveries of 1,5‑AG in human serum sample were 101.70%, 102.5% and 103.37%, respectively. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was ranged from 4.325% to 6.782%. The results imply that the proposed colorimetric method could be effectively applied in the clinical determination of the 1,5‑AG levels in serum samples.

A simple, colorimetric 1,5‑AG detection system has been proposed based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction. Herein, 1,5‑AG was oxidized to 1,5‑anhydrofuctose and H2O2 under the catalytic action of PROD. The GQDs, owing to their intrinsic peroxidase-like activity, catalyzed the H2O2-mediated oxidation peroxidase substrate TMB, resulting a color change. In the range of 20.0–100.0μg/mL, a linear curve was obtained (Y=0.000547X+0.01809) between absorbance and 1,5‑AG concentrations. The LOD for 1,5‑AG detection was 0.144μg/mL. The proposed 1,5‑AG detection system based on GQDs and PROD-catalyzed reaction showed better performances with simple operation, low-cost, higher selectivity. In the future, according to the direct relationship of the color shade with the amount of 1,5‑AG in the analytes, 1,5‑AG detection strip might be fabricated using the proposed system in clinical diagnosis.
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest to disclose.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81760534 and 81460451), the National Science Foundation of Guangxi province of China (Nos. 2015GXNSFDA139025, 2016GXNSFAA380011 and 2016GXNSFAA380080), the Appropriate Health Technology Development Project of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (No. 201422-03) and the Fund of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Metabolic Diseases Research (Nos. 2015-181h-01 and 2015-181h-02).
